turns.text: -- in practice, is that you cannot suspend the cause of action.  You could suspend the cause of action, nobody can be sued.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: If you don't suspend --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Including private parties.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Including private parties.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So this is a suspension that doesn't just have to do with foreign sovereigns.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: This suspension goes way wider than that.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So it's not clear that the suspension has anything to do with FSIA rules.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: I don't think it did.  It had to do with the fact that the cause of action itself was very controversial, and the President --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And the -- and the amount of resources against which attachment could occur is vastly greater against private entities, right?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.  That's why the private entities were sued.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Because more of them, including other foreign governments, have more property in the U.S., correct?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: No question about that. But back to --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So the suspension, given what Justice Alito pointed out to, which is there's going to be very little assets of Cuban agency or the Cuban government in the U.S., most of the assets are going to be in the private lawsuits.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.  But back to Justice Gorsuch's question because I think it's central --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Before you do that, I don't want to interrupt the chain of -- of questioning, but I thought you told me -- at least -- well, you didn't tell me, but I thought you told us that the receipt by a U.S. importer of money from a Cuban instrumentality would fall within the commercial activity exception of the FSIA.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.  By a U.S. exporter of goods to Cuba --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yeah, a U.S. exporter.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: -- and they would receive money back in the United States.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Yeah.  Okay.  But then you just, you know, two minutes ago, one minute ago, you said there would be very little property here to be executed on.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Oh, no.  If it was against a Cuban entity.  If it's against a Cuban entity, they couldn't execute --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: -- but it would fall within the FSIA's commercial exception because it would be clearly a direct effect. But back to Justice Kavanaugh, they -- the text gives the President the power to suspend the cause of action against anybody, private entities, cruise lines, Cuban entities, or any third -- any third-country's entities. It does not give the President --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: So it's even broader.  I mean --

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: But it -- it -- it does not give the President the power to suspend immunity or to override immunity.  What the President could do is say:  No suits can be brought. The President does not have the power to say that once suits are brought, and if they are brought against a foreign government entity, I'm suspending the FSIA.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That's a fair point.  I just think it's structurally trying to figure out how this fits together is why I'm asking these questions.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Well, but that's the way to harm -- the provision allows for the suspension of the cause of action but doesn't give the President any power whatsoever about an immunity except --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And the point --

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: -- to prevent these causes of action.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Does -- does --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And the point I was making with the SG is that if it did, if the Congress's intention was to give that -- the President a toggle switch with respect to sovereign immunity, wouldn't we have expected to see that in the statute?  Because that's like an extraordinary thing.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Exactly, Your Honor.  And as Justice Jackson alludes to, Congress made a considered, comprehensive determination that we didn't want the President to be doing this because it led to all sorts of bad things.  I mean, all sorts of --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Just the -- just --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: That was the point of the FSIA to begin with, right?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Why do you think the presidential provision is in there given the FSIA?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Because -- because Title III itself was incredibly controversial.  And the President argued not just that the foreign sovereign immunities exception should not be in, but Title III shouldn't be in. And, actually, Warren Christopher, the Secretary of State, made his -- sent a letter to the House on September 20 excoriating Title III.  It's bad for this reason.  It's bad for that reason.  He didn't mention one reason, foreign sovereign immunity, and that's because they knew they took out the exception two months thereafter.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well, maybe that's also because, you know, the Cuba -- Cuba and its instrumentalities were the most culpable. I mean, so you're going to go after the private entities because that's where the money is, but maybe -- maybe Congress and the executive weren't as concerned about the sovereign immunity question when they were talking about Cuba.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Right.  But -- but the question isn't Cuba's culpability.  As the argument has shown, what Congress was interested in was deterring trade and transactions with Cuba. And if you look at the findings, 6081(5), (6), (7), (11), it's all about deterring third countries from entering into joint ventures, entering into trade with Cuba. And so that's what -- but I don't think, in any event --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well, because it didn't want Cuba to continue to profiteer and make money off of the property that it had expropriated.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: But that says nothing about sovereign immunity.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Yeah.  I mean, I'm not -- well, I'm not fighting with you about that point.  I'm just saying that I don't think this whole reliance on the Christopher statement to the House does that much for you, but anyway.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Well, no.  I'm just -- I used the Christopher statement only to rebut the argument that Congress still must have thought or that the President still thought that there was an abrogation of immunity. The fact that Christopher never mentioned that three months later after they took out the exception meant that the President no longer thought there was an abrogation of immunity and, therefore, didn't argue against it at that point.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Just to be clear where we stand, you've made some statements during your argument that seem to me to suggest that you think that the mere fact that Congress did not say expressly that it was abrogating sovereign immunity is sufficient, that's the end of it?  We don't need to look any further than that?  Is that your argument?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: No.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: You --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So then the question is what we can infer from what we see in the statute, right --

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: I don't think so.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- in the -- in the relevant context?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: No, I don't think it's what you can infer.  I think there's two ways that you've said in Kirtz and FOMB that you can abrogate immunity and that you can abrogate the FSIA, either some express language, which you don't have, or providing a cause of action against a government -- government entities -- in this case, it would be foreign government entities -- which would be rendered a dead letter, which would be rendered meaningless. We --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, that's an interesting reading of Kirtz, but there's nothing in the opinion that talks to that.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Well, certainly, there's something in FOMB, which Kirtz relies on, which says that --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Kirtz itself doesn't say anything about that, does it?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: It says "negate."  And I think that reading Kirtz in any other way would both be inconsistent with your precedent and would leave this Court in all of those situations down -- down a path of determining what's not a dead letter?  Is it like in the expropriation exception, is it that there are 1 percent of claims that can succeed?  How are you going to determine that?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, if we knew that Congress thought that the immunity conferred by the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act would wipe out 99.999 percent of the potential claims that could be asserted under Helms-Burton, wouldn't it be a fair inference that it did not intend to -- to preserve the foreign sovereign immunities immunity?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: I think that the text governs because you can't determine whether it would knock out 99.99 percent.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, what's the difference between a hundred and 99.9 or whatever?  I mean --

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: -- in the case of a hundred, you're inferring they wouldn't have -- they wouldn't have created a cause of action against a -- an instrument -- a foreign government instrumentality if it was a dead letter.  It's an inference. And I don't understand why the inference stops the minute you can find one in a million claims that might get through.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Even if I agreed with you that -- that you can make that inference, that's not the case here.  There are many claims that could --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, first of all, do -- do you agree with Justice Alito we can make that --

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: I do not.  I do not.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: So we can only make that inference if a hundred percent of cases are eliminated?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: If you look at the statute and you look at the two -- and you look at the FSIA and you look at Title III --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: No, no, no.  I'm asking -- forget about the FSIA.  We've -- we've dealt with that.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: We're just dealing with -- with what -- what's required to abrogate sovereign immunity, and you're saying: Okay, you can either use the magic words, or you can create a cause of action that would completely be useless otherwise.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Zero percent of cases.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: You agree with those two things?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: I do.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: But, once we get to 99.9 percent of cases, you say that same inference is unavailable.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Because I think it's impossible to tell, looking at the statutes, how many -- not in -- not in this case, but more generally, whether or not -- how many cases are too few, whether or not they're going to succeed. If you could say we know for certain that 99.9 percent of all the cases are going to fail, maybe then.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: But you'll never know for certain.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay, okay, okay. So now we're at 99.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.  How about 90?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: That's exactly my point. That is at the --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: No, but you conceded 99.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: No, no.  That -- that's why I didn't concede 99.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, you didn't at first.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: I said maybe.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Oh, maybe.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Maybe.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Oh, maybe.  Okay. All right. (Laughter.)

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: But the reason that I don't want to concede 99 is because then you'd say what about 90?  And the answer to that is the answer that Justice Scalia gave in the NML case.  If you think that too many cases are being excluded, go to Congress.  Get Congress to enact a new statute.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Well, then it's magic --

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Don't amend this statute.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Then we're magic words, right?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: No.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: It --

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: I can give -- I can give you an example where the FSIA -- Congress could provide a cause of action without explicitly abrogating the FSIA and it should be abrogated under Kirtz. Let's say there was a spate of automobile accidents in some country or some reason in the -- region of the world where all the instrumentalities -- all the automobile accidents were caused by foreign instrumentalities, and Congress enacted a statute, there was some ether going around in the Congress, and they forgot that the FSIA existed, which clearly isn't here.  They were staring the FSIA in the face. But let's say they said there will be a cause of action for any American national injured in an automobile accident that occurs in another country.  That would be barred by 1605 -- 16 -- that would be barred by the FSIA's tort exception because it requires that the tort occur in the United States.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Mr. Lobel, isn't the real reason why we have to keep the Kirtz avenue narrow is because, if you don't do that, you end up end-running Congress's intentions with respect to enacting the FSIA?  That we have a statute that Congress, as Justice Kagan said at the beginning, thought about the universe of claims that could be brought against foreign governments, said we don't want this being done in an ad hoc fashion anymore, so we're going to create a statute that lays out what we, the Court, has said subsequently is the exclusive way in which you get to sue a foreign government. Kirtz comes along and we see this kind of one circumstance in which we might conceive of an exception to application of the statute that Congress has created for this purpose, and it is this dead letter, you know, if you have a cause of action, but there's no way you could ever, you know, abrogate sovereign immunity -- or a cause of action against a government, but you could never recover.  Then you go, okay, that really does have to be interpreted as a situation in which Congress did intend for sovereign immunity to be abrogated, notwithstanding the FSIA. But, if you get broader than that, if we're in 99 percent of the cases, 97 percent, 96, then you could have a rule operating outside of the FSIA to do the very thing that Congress didn't want the FSIA -- or didn't want to have happen, which is why it enacted the FSIA.  So this exception has to be, like, really tiny or else we don't give effect to Congress's expressed intent to have the FSIA be the exclusive avenue for abrogating sovereign immunity.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Exactly, Your Honor. Exactly.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, the FSIA may -- may have been the exclusive avenue when the FSIA was enacted.  But the Congress that enacted the FSIA does not have the right to control what later Congresses do.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: So I don't know where that --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And that's why --

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I don't know where that -- I don't -- I --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- that's why we -- that's Justice Gorsuch's point, right?  Later Congresses can say with respect to each statute this -- don't apply the FSIA here.  And they can say that.  But what -- what's happening here is that we are now implying that later Congresses intended that.  But that -- that language doesn't appear in this statute, but we're having a two-hour argument as to whether or not we should interpret it, and -- and I don't understand that.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Right.  And it's not any --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice --

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: -- magic words.  They just need to --

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I think Justice Alito had a question.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Oh, sure.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: No, that's all right. Go ahead.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Well, just one last point. They claim that you can harmonize the statute, the two statutes, by reading a tiny exception into the FSIA.  And I agree with Justice Jackson that once you start reading that tiny exception in, it -- it will expand.  The camel's tent -- nose will be under the tent. But that does not harmonize these two statutes.  The rule that specific can -- can override a general in this context doesn't apply when there's an implied repeal question. Their reading of the statute is that you have a narrow exception, a narrow repeal of the FSIA, and that's still an implied repeal. As this Court said in Home Builders, it doesn't matter whether you call it an exception, an amendment, an implied amendment; it's still an implied repeal. And it contradicts the FSIA.  The FSIA 1604, paragraph 16 -- Section 1604 says foreign states shall be immune unless one of the exceptions apply.  They want to read the statute exactly how, Justice Alito, you didn't allow them to -- to read the statute in Home Builders.  Just add another exception.  Just add another criteria which is not in the statute.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: In 1996, were -- in 1996, were there instrumentalities of the Cuban government that were engaging in commercial activity in the United States?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: There definitely were. You don't have to take my word for it. Secretary -- the Under Secretary of State Peter Tarnoff testified before the Senate committee dealing with this exact statute, the Helms-Burton Act, and said, in many respects, the trade with Cuba is substantial. And he was right.  There were $65 million of medicines and medical supplies.  Not just humanitarian goods.  Medicines, medical supplies.  There were 20 -- over 20,000 travelers in 1993, 50,000 travelers in 1994. And there were a quarter of a billion dollars according to Exxon's testimony -- exhibits in the district court of remittances going to Cuban entities in 1994, and as the court of appeals held here, those remittances could be the basis of lawsuits under Title III. And I don't think you can fix it to 1995/1996 in any event because the statute gives the President the power to expand trade with Cuba.  And so whatever the case was in 1995, given that Congress gave the President the power to expand trade, they must have anticipated that trade might be expanded. In fact, in Section 112 of the statute, it explicitly anticipates that the remittance policy will be expanded.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further?

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Just so I'm clear, pre-FSIA, could Title III work as it's written?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Pre-FSIA?

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: It could because pre-FSIA, 1952 to 1976, it could because there was a restrictive regime of immunity, which wouldn't have rendered Title III suits meaningless. Prior to 1952, it probably wouldn't -- it probably meets the Kirtz -- the Kirtz exception.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So this is basically a statutory case?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: It is clearly a statutory case.  You've got to interpret these two statutes harmoniously.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Alito? Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Under Kirtz's language, you either have an express waiver or -- but a -- but a implied waiver of sovereign immunity that is "unmistakably clear" in the language of the statute. And then what Kirtz says to -- when there's a cause of action against a government on a claim, it has to be clear enough to effectively "negate a claim Congress has clearly authorized," correct?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Mm-hmm.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're relying on the negation question?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And my colleague said, well, doesn't the amount of the negation -- how much is enough?  And the point is, however, you're saying we don't know how much, but there's a meaningful amount, correct?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: This is not 1 percent?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: It's not 1 percent.  In any event, it was a hypothetical question.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: But, here, there are hundreds of claims, thousands of claims probably, that could be brought.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And if you go as far as Petitioner wants, there's hundreds of, maybe thousands of claims against foreign governments and foreign entities, correct?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Yes.  The statute says -- doesn't say Cuban -- claims against Cuban entities.  It says any instrumentality of a foreign state -- of a foreign state.  That's the text the statute.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So we could be expanding this even more?

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Correct.  You --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: You'd be allowing plaintiffs to sue every instrumentality in the world that does any trade with Cuba.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch, anything? Justice Kavanaugh? Justice Barrett? Justice Jackson? Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: JULES L. LOBEL
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Rebuttal, Ms. Ratner?

turns.name: MORGAN L. RATNER
turns.text: Thank you.  Four points. First, Justice Alito, to your question about why this practically matters, the first is yes, there are not significant assets of Cuban instrumentalities in the United States. We have the ability to seek those assets worldwide, which is pretty standard fare in dealing with international judgments. The second is that Exxon is a company that plays the long game.  We've had this claim for over 60 years now, and so there may be changes in the future that affect what assets we can recover on. Second point, I hate to complicate the presidential suspension authority further, but I do want to be clear that there are two suspension authorities that are specific to the Cuban government -- excuse me, to Cuban instrumentalities, that's 6064(a) and 6082(d), so this can't be dismissed as just potentially suspensions relating to private entities' claims. Third point.  I think the food and medicine importer -- importer examples really underscore the extreme mismatch here of a Congress that shut down commerce and allowed a few humanitarian examples -- food imports, family-oriented remittances, medicine imports -- and now, under Respondents' theory, those are the only hook.  Those are the only claims that you can bring against Cuban instrumentalities, are the very thing that Congress decided to have a little more solicitude for.  I think that creates an incredible mismatch with what they were trying to do here. Finally, I -- I want to underscore we are not asking for this Court to have some broad rule that once a sovereign is covered by a cause of action, that's enough. Again, we have pointed to all of these overlapping provisions.  This is the express and only defendant called out.  There are rules specific to these claims and judgments.  There are multiple overlapping suspension authorities.  And there are jurisdictional provisions that take this claim out of the So that is the sum total, and that is enough for a background statute like the FSIA, just as it would be enough for a background constitutional or common law immunity protection.  Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
